1:
The chapter opens with a family register, and the witnesses agree that nothing in it is idle. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the elaborate genealogy of the Benjamite Kish and the minute description of his son are intended to indicate, at the very outset, the importance Saul would attain in relation to the people of Israel. Where 1 Chronicles 8:33 says that Ner begat Kish, while this verse and 1 Samuel 14:51 make Kish the son of Abiel, they reconcile the accounts “in the simplest manner” by taking Ner for a grandfather or still remoter ancestor, intervening links being freely passed over in Hebrew pedigrees. Ellicott reaches the same verdict from the scribal side: each genealogical summary omits the names not needed for its purpose, and he adds Dean Payne Smith’s suggestion that the “hopeless entanglement” of the Benjamite genealogies owes much to the terrible civil war of Judges 20, in whose massacres many of the tribe’s older records must have perished.

A small but real disagreement concerns “a mighty man of power.” Keil and Delitzsch insist the Hebrew signifies “not a brave man, but a man of property,” as in Ruth 2:1, where the same phrase describes the wealthy Boaz. Adam Clarke takes it literally — “a strong man; this appears to be the only power he possessed” — and thinks the father’s physical strength accounts for the extraordinary size of the son. Matthew Henry characteristically declines to choose: Kish was “in spirit bold, in body strong, in estate wealthy,” and since the whole territory of Benjamin had fallen to a mere six hundred survivors, their inheritances were the larger for their fewness.

Henry draws the richest theology from the tribal name itself. Saul was of Benjamin, as was the New Testament Saul who became Paul and counted the descent his honour (Rom. 11:1; Phil. 3:5). The tribe had been all but annihilated in the fatal war with Gibeah, so that Israel’s first king “sprang as a root out of a dry ground”: the tribe fewest in number was first in royal dignity, “God giving more abundant honour to that part which lacked” (1 Cor. 12:24). Gill notes simply that from Jacob’s youngest son came Israel’s first king. Ellicott, surveying the whole episode before expounding it, calls it “singularly picturesque”: the yet unproclaimed king throwing his whole powers into the everyday business of his father’s farm; the reverent, perhaps slightly reluctant admiration of the old seer; the symbol gifts and burning words that “woke up the sleeping hero-spirit”; and a Saul who returned to the ancestral farm without vulgar elation, subject to his father as in old days, until the public summons came. Together the witnesses read the dry genealogy as the first line of a providential drama: God begins the story of Israel’s monarchy not at a court but on a farm, in the smallest tribe, with a family register of forgotten names.
2:
The witnesses linger over the one thing the historian tells us about Saul: his looks. Ellicott begins with the grammar. The Hebrew behind “a choice young man, and a goodly” cannot bear both epithets; the translators leaned on the Vulgate’s electus, avoiding the plain sense “young” because Saul already had in Jonathan a son near manhood. Translate simply “a young man,” he says — young in the full vigour of manhood, some forty to forty-five years old, as contrasted with the aged Samuel. He then sets the description in the ancient world: “in the childhood of nations heroic proportions were highly valued,” and he lines up Herodotus, Aristotle, Virgil’s Turnus and Homer’s Ajax to show that Saul’s gigantic stature and manly beauty “no doubt contributed to the ready acceptance” of the king by a still semi-barbarous Israel. Clarke agrees that “it was probably from this very circumstance that he was chosen,” since wherever kings were elective great respect was paid to personal appearance, and Wesley adds that a tall stature was much valued in a king “in ancient times, and in the eastern countries.”

Matthew Henry turns the same observation into a quiet indictment. No mention is made of Saul’s wisdom or virtue, his learning or piety, “or any of the accomplishments of his mind,” only that he had a good face, a good shape and a good presence. When God chose a king after his own heart he pitched upon David, remarkable for nothing in his countenance but innocence and sweetness (1 Sam. 16:7); when he chose a king after the people’s heart, who aimed at stateliness and grandeur, he pitched upon this huge tall man who, “if he had no other good qualities, yet would look great.” Henry even suspects the height concealed no answering strength: Samson had real might, and Israel slighted and betrayed him; “justly therefore are they now put off with one who, though of uncommon height, is weak as other men.” They would have a king like the nations, and the nations commonly chose portly men.

Gill supplies the New Testament counterpoint: the great apostle of the Gentiles bore this same name and sprang from this same tribe, “but very different in stature; he was a little man, this a large tall man.” The irony is left for the reader to savour — the tall Saul of Benjamin failed, the little Saul of Benjamin turned the world upside down. So the witnesses divide less over the facts than over their weight: the grammatical-historical expositors see a providentially fitting credential for a rough age, while the Puritan reads the same verse as the first hint of the tragedy to come — a people who asked for a king their eyes could admire received exactly what they asked, head and shoulders of him, and no more.
3:
All the witnesses are struck by the smallness of the hinge on which the kingdom turns. Matthew Henry gives the theme its classic form: here is “a great man rising from small beginnings” and “a great event arising from small occurrences.” Saul held no office and enjoyed no preferment; from a level with his neighbours he stepped at once into the throne, according to Hannah’s song, “He raiseth up the poor out of the dust, to set them among princes” (1 Sam. 2:8). How low the history begins — the future king is introduced hunting strayed she-asses. Clarke marvels at the same providence from the other side: “what a wonderful train of occurrences were connected in order to bring Saul to the throne of Israel! Every thing seems to go on according to the common course of events, and yet all conspired” to the election. Yet Clarke adds a darker clause the others do not: they conspired to favour the election of a man “who certainly did not come there by the approbation of God” — the providence is real, but it is the providence of a concession, God giving in judgment the king he had granted in anger. Henry, by contrast, keeps the accent on sovereignty rather than displeasure: “promotion comes not by chance nor human probabilities, but God is the Judge.”

The errand itself the witnesses refuse to despise. Wesley notes that asses were “of great price, because of the scarcity of horses, and therefore not held unworthy of Saul’s seeking,” in those ancient times “when simplicity, humility, and industry were in fashion among persons of quality.” Ellicott fills in the picture: the horse was forbidden by the law, and asses carried judges and nobles — the white asses of Judges 5:10, the thirty colts of Jair’s thirty sons (Judges 10:4). Kish’s beasts, kept untethered for breeding, simply strayed. Ellicott also lingers on the unnamed companion: not a slave but a trusted “servant,” familiar with his young master, keeper of the money, welcomed by Samuel at the feast — traditionally identified, he notes, with Doeg the Edomite of 1 Samuel 22:18.

Henry draws the household lessons with relish: those who have must expect to lose; it is wisdom to look after what is lost; no man should think it below him to know the state of his flocks; and children should be forward to serve their parents’ interests. Saul’s care of the asses he credits “not so much to the humility of his spirit as to the plainness and simplicity of those times,” but his obedience to his father was very commendable — the diligent, dutiful man “stands fair for preferment.” The shared conclusion is the one Henry states best: Saul missed what he sought and had no reason to complain of the disappointment, “for he met with the kingdom, which he never dreamed of.”
4:
The itinerary of the fruitless search is traced with care by the geographers among the witnesses. Keil and Delitzsch lay out the route: the chain of the mountains of Ephraim runs southward into the tribe-territory of Benjamin, where the patrimony of Kish lay; Saul and his servant swept through that hill-country, then the land of Shalisha, then the land of Shaalim, then Benjamite ground again, and found nothing. Ellicott attempts the place-names. Shalisha is the “land of the Three,” so called, he suggests, because three valleys there united in one — the region of Baal-shalisha (2 Kings 4:42), some fifteen miles north of Lydda; Shalim is probably “a very deep valley,” its name derived from a Hebrew word signifying “the hollow of the hand.” Gill is content with the practical point: they began with the mountainous part of Ephraim because it lay contiguous to Benjamin, “where it might be supposed the asses had strayed.”

Behind the geography the witnesses see a design. The search is long — three days, as v.20 will reveal — and systematic, quarter after quarter combed and abandoned, and every stage of it is a failure. Yet each failure bends the travellers’ path, without their knowledge, toward the land of Zuph and the city of the seer. Matthew Henry’s observation on the episode belongs here: Saul and his servant travelled far, probably on foot, in quest of the asses, “but in vain: they found them not” — and the disappointment was itself the appointment. Clarke’s wonder at the “train of occurrences” that carried Saul to the throne rests precisely on details like these: nothing in the verse rises above the common course of events, two tired men zig-zagging through border country after livestock, and yet the whole map is being drawn by another hand.

There is a pastoral fitness, the witnesses imply, in the fact that Scripture preserves the dead ends. The narrative might have begun at Ramah; instead it walks us through Shalisha and Shaalim, districts remembered for nothing except that the asses were not there. The commentators’ patient labour over these obscure names is its own small sermon: the stretches of life that seem mere wasted road — the searches that turn up nothing, the provinces passed through and left behind — are recorded in God’s book, because they are the very route by which he brings a man, weary and empty-handed, to the door where his calling waits. Saul at the end of this verse has nothing to show for his journey; he is, in fact, three days nearer his crown.
5:
At the land of Zuph the search is called off, and the witnesses find in Saul’s reason for turning back the first touch of his character. Gill identifies the district: this is the region of Ramathaim-zophim, Samuel’s native soil, and the Targum renders it simply “the land in which was the prophet” — as though the geography itself whispered what the travellers did not yet know. Ellicott places it in the south-west of Benjamin, and Clarke reports Calmet’s reconstruction of the whole circuit: from Gibeah to Shalisha, thence to Shalim near Jerusalem, then back through Benjamin into Zuph — in all about twenty-five leagues, a journey of some three days on foot. The exhausted pair are at the far edge of their loop, provisions spent, when Saul says, “Come, and let us return; lest my father leave caring for the asses, and take thought for us.”

On that sentence the witnesses converge warmly. Ellicott quotes Dr. Kitto: “Saul’s tender regard for his father’s feelings here is a favourable indication of character.” Keil and Delitzsch draw out the Hebrew idiom — the father will “desist from” the asses, give them up, and begin to be anxious about the son and servant instead; Saul can calculate the moment at which, in Kish’s heart, the lost beasts will be displaced by the lost boy. Matthew Henry develops the point into a household commonplace worth keeping: Saul acted “in consideration of his father’s tender concern for him,” apprehensive that the aged Kish would fear, as Jacob feared for Joseph, that some evil beast had devoured them or some mischief befallen them. “Children,” Henry concludes, “should take care that they do nothing to grieve or frighten their parents, but be tender of their tenderness.”

The witnesses are also quietly instructed by where the story has brought its hero. Saul proposes to go home empty-handed — a sensible, dutiful, entirely unremarkable decision — and it is exactly this decision that providence uses, for had he turned back one district earlier he would never have stood within reach of Ramah. The son’s thoughtfulness for his father becomes the occasion of his meeting the prophet who will give him a new father-figure and a new house. There is a foreshadowing here that several witnesses feel without pressing: the man who begins by fearing to add grief to Kish will one day be the grief of Israel; but at this hour, on the road in Zuph with his mind on an old man’s anxiety, Saul is at his best, and Scripture has been careful to show him so before the crown finds him.
6:
The proposal to consult Samuel comes not from Saul but from his servant, and the witnesses make much of both halves of that fact. Matthew Henry observes dryly that the servant, “it should seem, had more religion in him than his master”; Saul, who lived within twenty miles of Ramah, must be told by his hired man that “there is in this city a man of God.” Ellicott is charmed rather than critical: the young countryman “in the simplicity of his heart” thinks the loss of his master’s asses a sufficient occasion to intrude on the prophet-judge of Israel — and that very simplicity, Ellicott argues, is historical evidence. The relation between Samuel and the people must have been “of a very close and friendly nature,” else it would never have occurred to a farm-servant to seek so great a man over so small a matter; “it says much for the old prophet’s kindly, unselfish disposition that his name was thus loved and honoured, even in the secluded farms of the Land of Promise.”

The servant’s description of Samuel the witnesses weigh word by word. He is “an honourable man” — better, says Ellicott, “one held in honour.” Wesley defines the honour: “one of great reputation for his skill and faithfulness,” and turns it into a maxim — “acquaintance with God and serviceableness to the kingdom of God makes men truly honourable.” Henry says the same: men of God are honourable men, and should be so in our eyes. The credential offered is precise: “all that he saith cometh surely to pass,” the very mark stamped on Samuel from his youth, when God “did let none of his words fall to the ground” (1 Sam. 3:19). Wesley notes the modesty of the “peradventure he can shew us our way”: the servant doubts whether so great a prophet would seek, or God would grant, a revelation “concerning such mean matters,” though God sometimes condescended to his people in small things precisely “to cut off all pretence or occasion of seeking to heathenish divination.”

Yet Henry will not let the errand pass without a rebuke that has become famous. To ask the seer nothing but the road to the asses was “a poor business to employ a prophet about.” Had they said, let us give up the asses for lost and go learn from him the good knowledge of God, the proposal would have become Israelites; instead they make prophecy, “that glory of Israel, serve so mean a turn.” And then the sentence every generation has repeated: “most people would rather be told their fortune than told their duty, how to be rich than how to be saved.” The witnesses thus agree on the double lesson of the verse: wherever we are, as Henry says, we should improve our opportunities of acquainting ourselves with the wise and good — and we should bring them questions worthy of the God they serve.
7:
Saul’s scruple — “what shall we bring the man?” — opens a genuine question among the witnesses: is this the etiquette of the East or the instinct of a man who thinks prophecy can be bought? Clarke defends Saul entirely. “We are not to suppose from this that the prophets took money to predict future events”; Saul refers to “an invariable custom, that no man approached a superior without a present of some kind,” and Clarke garnishes the point from Ward’s observations of Bengal, where a suppliant brings fruits or sweetmeats and is wounded if they are refused. Ellicott agrees that the gift was “an act of respectful homage to a superior rather than a mere fee,” citing Jacob’s little present of spicery to the vizier of Egypt (Gen. 43:11) and the Queen of Sheba’s gifts to Solomon (1 Kings 10:10). Wesley lists the honest uses of such presents: a testimony of respect, a grateful acknowledgement, or support for the prophets, the sons of the prophets, and the poor known to them.

Gill lets a shadow of doubt fall on Saul himself. The Targum reads, “if he receives money,” and Gill observes that Saul genuinely did not know whether Samuel took fees — some who set up for prophets, “diviners and fortune tellers,” did — a telling ignorance in a man who had lived his whole life a day’s walk from Israel’s judge. Matthew Henry preserves both readings side by side. Some think Saul “measured him by himself, or by his sons,” as if Samuel must be hired like the false prophets who divined for money (Mic. 3:11), coming to him “as a fortune-teller rather than as a prophet” and pricing him accordingly. But Henry inclines to the kinder view: the usage was general and agreeable to natural equity, “that those who sowed spiritual things should reap temporal things from those for whom they are employed.”

On Samuel’s side all are agreed. He needed not their money, Henry says, and would not have denied his advice had they brought nothing; probably he gave such gifts to the poor; yet he did not refuse it, for it honoured his office. Keil and Delitzsch keep the narrative thread taut: the objection about the empty bread-vessels is simply the last obstacle providence must clear, and the servant clears it. The practical divinity the witnesses draw is double. Towards God’s servants, the instinct to come with full hands is right and ancient — even God, Clarke remarks, “would not that his people should approach him with empty hands.” But the minister of God must be such a man that the question “does he take money?” answers itself; Samuel’s forty years of incorruptible judgeship, remembered in 1 Samuel 12:3, are the standing rebuke to every hireling who has made the question necessary.
8:
The servant’s answer settles the difficulty with a coin, and the witnesses turn the little piece of silver over with antiquarian delight. Ellicott quotes Dean Payne Smith’s conjecture that “this shekel of silver was roughly stamped, and divided into four quarters by a cross, and broken when needed” — money in Samuel’s day was bullion, weighed and snapped, not coined — and adds that its proportionate value is beyond recovery, “for silver then was rare.” Clarke prices the fourth part of a shekel at about ninepence, and Matthew Henry at seven-pence halfpenny; both agree the sum is trifling, which is precisely the point. Since the bread was spent, Clarke reasons, a portion of the provisions would otherwise have served; “therefore the present was intended more as a token of respect than as an emolument.” No man fees a fortune-teller with a quarter-shekel; a poor traveller pays his homage with what he has, and, as Henry says elsewhere of small offerings, it was the widow’s mite.

Gill notices the structure of the scene: this is the second time the servant “answered Saul again.” It was the servant who knew of the man of God, the servant who urged the visit, and now the servant who has “here at hand” the fourth part of a shekel when his master’s purse is apparently empty. Keil and Delitzsch record the transaction without ornament — the objection is met, the last hindrance removed — but Henry presses the contrast between the two men with his usual edge. Saul, he suspects, mentioned the want of a present as an objection against going at all, “and would not own that he had money in his pocket”; only when the servant “generously offered to be at the charge” did he warm to the scheme. “Most people love a cheap religion,” Henry concludes, “and like it best when they can devolve the expense of it on others.”

The witnesses find in the verse a parable of unequal instruments. The future king of Israel is led to his anointing by a nameless servant’s piety, initiative and pocket-money; every resource for the decisive turn of the story is supplied from below. There is comfort and warning in it together. Comfort, because God is pleased to hang great events on the faithfulness of obscure people — the quarter-shekel in a farm-servant’s scrip is as much a link in the chain of providence as the word of the Lord in the prophet’s ear. Warning, because Saul’s passivity here — needing to be persuaded toward the man of God, and subsidised when he got there — is, for Henry at least, an early symptom of the spiritual inertness that would undo him when the kingdom was on his shoulders and there was no servant at hand to supply what the king lacked.
9:
The historian pauses to gloss his own vocabulary — “he that is now called a Prophet was beforetime called a Seer” — and the witnesses treat the parenthesis as a window into the making of the sacred text. Clarke is blunt: “this passage could not have been a part of this book originally.” Samuel or a contemporary wrote memoranda; a later author compiled the book; the explanatory note betrays the compiler’s hand — and Clarke even thinks the verse would sit more naturally after v.11. Ellicott agrees that the verse “was evidently inserted in the original book of memoirs by a later hand,” perhaps a scribe of Ezra’s day, but insists he was “a reviser of the sacred text of very high authority,” since the note has come down as an integral part of the received Hebrew. Wesley keeps a second door open: the words belong “either to some later sacred writer” or to Samuel himself, who, writing at fifty or sixty of his young days, “might well call it before time.” The debate is real but bounded — none of the witnesses finds the note anything but inspired and useful.

On the words themselves Ellicott gives the fullest account. Three Hebrew terms name the inspired man: roeh, the seer, the most ancient, the title by which Samuel was known in his lifetime — “Is the seer here?”, “I am the seer”; chozeh, the gazer on visions, which succeeded it; and nabi, the prophet, which began to come into common use in Samuel’s own time. The scribe’s purpose was pastoral in its way: by his day roeh no longer meant a man of God, and he wished readers to know that when Samuel lived the old word had not yet deteriorated. Clarke supplies the semantics: the seer is one who sees, “particularly preternatural sights”; “a seer was always a prophet, but the prophet was not always a seer”; and he ranges from Abraham, the first man called prophet (Gen. 20:7), through Balaam with his eyes open (Num. 24:4), to Virgil’s Sibyl crying cerno — “I view” — to show that all antiquity, true religion and false, spoke of revelation as sight.

Gill anchors the idiom: to “inquire of God” is, in the Targum’s phrase, to “ask doctrine,” to seek the mind of God in a weighty affair through a man eminent for piety and the spirit of prophecy. Wesley’s definition is the simplest and serves for them all: the seer was so called “because he discerned and could discover things secret and unknown to others.” The witnesses’ shared conclusion gives the verse its devotional weight: names for the ministry may shift with the centuries, but the substance is constant — Israel’s teacher must be first a man to whom God shows himself. As Matthew Henry puts it in his comment on this passage, those that are prophets must first be seers; “those who undertake to speak to others of the things of God must have an insight into those things themselves.”
10:
“Well said; come, let us go.” Gill hears in the Hebrew — literally “good is thy word” — Saul’s frank approval of the whole scheme: the proposal is sound, the servant generous to give all he has to the man, and the prospect promising, “that since he is a seer he may inform us where the asses are, or which way we must take to find them. Things look feasible enough.” It is the speech of a practical man weighing a practical errand; nothing in it suspects that the road up to the city is the road to a throne. Matthew Henry, reviewing the exchange, cannot resist the observation that Saul “never thought of going to the man of God till the servant proposed it,” and consented only when the charge was to be borne by another; the future head of Israel is still, spiritually, being led by the hand.

The commentators then ask what city this was, since the narrative — almost teasingly — withholds the name. Ellicott argues the case for Ramah, Samuel’s own town. The impression the whole account leaves is of the seer’s usual residence: Samuel had built an altar to the Lord there (1 Sam. 7:17), and on the day of Saul’s arrival a great sacrifice was proceeding on the high place; in this nameless city the seer had a house of his own (vv.18, 25); and Samuel was known to Saul’s servant as dwelling precisely here. Keil and Delitzsch carry the travellers forward without pausing over the question: the two went up toward the town where the man of God was, the narrative already hastening to the meeting for whose sake every previous verse was written.

Slight as the verse is, the witnesses find its structure eloquent. Saul’s “let us go” is spoken in complete ignorance; the reader, who has watched the search fail through four districts, knows that the words carry the speaker toward an appointment fixed in heaven a day before (v.15). Providence, they observe, does not need informed consent to accomplish its designs: it is content with a tired man’s reasonable assent to his servant’s sensible suggestion. And there is a gentle irony, noted since Gill, in Saul’s hope that the seer will tell him “which way we must take.” That is exactly what Samuel will do — though the way in question will prove to be not the road back to Gibeah, but the way of a man on whom the desire of all Israel has quietly come to rest. The ordinary decision, ordinarily made, is the door into the extraordinary purpose of God.
11:
On the hill road to the city the travellers meet young maidens going out to draw water, and the witnesses relish the scene’s unforced realism. Gill notes the topography that makes it possible: the city was built upon a hill — hence its name Ramah, “high and lifted up” — so the strangers climb while the girls descend to the well below. Clarke draws his small apologetic lesson: “so far is it from being true that young women were always kept closely shut up at home, that we find them often in the field, drawing and carrying water, as here” — the daughters of Ramah move freely about their homely business, like Rebekah and Rachel before them.

Matthew Henry pauses first over what the meeting proves about Saul. Gibeah lay not twenty miles from Ramah, and Samuel often judged Israel at neighbouring Mizpeh; yet Saul had lived so very privately, and taken so little notice of public affairs, that he had never seen Samuel — when they meet in v.18 he will not know him by face. Henry turns even this to account: “so that there was no cause to suspect any secret compact or collusion between them in this matter” — the choice of Saul cannot have been contrived between prophet and candidate, for the candidate could not have picked the prophet out of a crowd. He compares the Baptist’s word about Christ, “I knew him not” (John 1:31), though he adds that he does not think the ignorance any commendation to Saul.

Then Henry turns to the maidens, and finds in their ready, particular answer a sermon on religious neighbourhoods. Even the maid-servants of Ramah could tell the strangers of the sacrifice, of the seer’s movements, of the hour of the feast and the custom of the blessing; they knew the liturgy of their town as well as its gossip. He quotes Bishop Hall’s inference with approval: “where there is the practice and example of piety in the better sort, there will be a reflection of it upon the meanest. It is no small advantage to live in religious places; for we shall be much to blame if all goodness fall beside us.” And he adds his own counsel: we should always be ready to give what assistance we can to those that are enquiring after God’s prophets, and to further them in their enquiries. The witnesses together read the verse as providence working at its most domestic: the last relay in the chain that brings Saul to Samuel is a cluster of girls at a well, whose casual precision about “the seer” delivers the future king, on time, to the man who is waiting for him.
12:
The maidens’ answer tumbles out in eager detail — he is; behold, he is before you; make haste; he came to-day; there is a sacrifice of the people to-day in the high place — and Ellicott hears in its very breathlessness the mark of an eye-witness account: “the little scene, in itself in no way remarkable, is recounted evidently as introducing momentous consequences.” The girls of Ramah, “loving to chatter and exhibit their local knowledge” and plainly attached to their great fellow-citizen, point the strangers up toward the high place; and every word they spoke, he suggests, “was remembered in after time by Saul and his companion,” for whom that day divided life into before and after. Wesley explains the “came to-day”: Samuel had been travelling abroad — on his circuit, Matthew Henry supposes, or from his country seat — and was newly returned to his own house; his movements, being a public person’s, were generally known. Clarke adds the tradition that besides his home at Ramah Samuel kept a dwelling at Naioth, where a school of the prophets is later found (1 Sam. 19:18–24).

The “high place” detains the exegetes. Keil and Delitzsch insist on the word’s technical sense: bamah is not any height or hill, but “the high place, as a place of sacrifice or prayer.” Wesley locates it on the hill of v.11, near the altar Samuel had built; and Henry explains why worship there was no irregularity: the tabernacle, deprived of the ark, had lost its old standing, and until God should again choose a place to put his name in, other places were allowed — Samuel had built an altar at Ramah (1 Sam. 7:17) and here makes use of it. Clarke describes the institution itself: “a sacrifice of the people” means a great feast, the animals first offered to the Lord, their blood poured out before him, and then all the people feasting on the flesh.

What the witnesses jointly recover from the verse is the texture of a town organised around its worship. The strangers ask for a man; the answer they receive is a liturgical timetable — the sacrifice, the blessing, the feast, and the seer at the centre of it all. Ellicott elsewhere remarks how strongly, in these chapters, “the religion of the Eternal coloured almost all parts of the every-day life of Israel,” and this verse is his best evidence: waterpots and peace-offerings, girls’ chatter and the high place, woven into one fabric. Saul, hurrying up the hill to catch a fortune-teller before dinner, is in fact stepping into the ordered devotion of a community whose feast has, unknown to every guest but one, been prepared for him.
13:
The maidens’ instructions end with a piece of table-liturgy: the people will not eat until Samuel come, “because he doth bless the sacrifice; and afterwards they eat that be bidden.” The witnesses seize on the words as a precious fragment of early worship. Ellicott calls it “a very early instance of the devout practice among the Hebrews of asking a blessing on meals.” Keil and Delitzsch give the philology: the Hebrew verb here answers to the Greek eulogein, and refers to “the thanksgiving prayer offered before the sacrificial meal.” Wesley analyses the blessing into its parts: thanksgiving — for this was a thank-offering — and prayer to God for its acceptance. Clarke stresses the presidency of the man of God: “he alone can perform the religious rites which are used on this occasion,” and he illustrates the open table from Arab custom, where the people of the vicinity are bidden to come and share a sacrificial feast.

Matthew Henry, as ever, builds the doctrine. Considered as a common meal, the scene teaches “the great duty of craving a blessing upon our meat before we partake of it”: we cannot expect benefit from our food without that blessing, and have no reason to expect the blessing if we do not pray for it — thus we give glory to God as our benefactor and own our dependence upon him. Considered as a religious assembly, it teaches more: Samuel prayed over the sacrifice and offered up spiritual sacrifices with it, and only when the holy duties were performed did they eat — “let the soul first be served.” The guests “feasted in token of their reconciliation to God by virtue of the sacrifice, and their participation of the benefits of it,” which leads Henry to see in the blessed feast upon the peace-offering a shadow of the Christian eucharist, where the meal is likewise sanctified by word and prayer.

Gill adds the small notice that the seer’s house evidently stood near the gate by which they entered, since the girls promise they will find him “straightway.” But the weight of the verse, all agree, lies in the picture of a whole town waiting, hungry, around spread tables, until the man of God has given thanks. The people’s patience is itself a confession: the meal is not theirs until it has been acknowledged as God’s. The witnesses would have their readers learn the same order at humbler tables — first the blessing, then the bread — and learn too, from a community that would not eat without its prophet, how much the presence of one praying man can hallow the common life of a place.
14:
The travellers enter the city, and “behold, Samuel came out against them.” Keil and Delitzsch note the economy of the narrative: at the very moment Saul and his servant pass in, Samuel meets them on his way out to the high place, and before the meeting itself is described the historian breaks off to explain (vv.15–17) how it was no meeting of chance at all. The encounter that looks, on the street, like two parties brushing past each other at a gate has been arranged from heaven a day in advance. Gill simply marks the ascent — Saul and his servant went up the hill into Ramah — but the barest details now carry the weight of the appointment the reader knows is coming.

Ellicott surrenders his commentary here to Ewald’s famous description, which deserves the space he gives it. Saul comes before Samuel “bashfully pursuing his humble quest, in apparent unconsciousness of the power slumbering within him”; the great seer receives him “in a way quite different from all that he could have hoped or feared.” Samuel at once desires to take him along to the sacrificial height, telling him beforehand how unimportant was the immediate object of his inquiry — the matter of the asses was already settled — “but that for him and his whole house was reserved a very different and far better destiny in Israel.” And when Saul, “in his unassuming simplicity, would fain waive the honour which is obscurely hinted — so little does he yet know his better self — the holy man, more discerning, takes him with him to the sacrificial meal,” seats him above the thirty invited guests, and has set before him a portion put by expressly for him: “for in like manner a portion other and higher than that of ordinary men had been long reserved for him by heaven.” The reserved dish becomes, in Ewald’s reading, the sacrament of a reserved destiny.

The witnesses find in this street-corner meeting the chapter’s doctrine in miniature. On one side walks a man who knows nothing — not the city’s name in the telling, not the seer’s face, not the errand’s true object; on the other, a man who knows everything, briefed by the word of the Lord and already host of a feast prepared for a guest he has never seen. Between the two moves the providence that has steered every strayed ass and failed search of the preceding week. As Matthew Henry remarks of this juncture, the meeting “seemed purely accidental, but the divine providence ordered it for the forwarding of this great event”: the wise God serves very great and certain purposes by small and casual occurrences, and a sparrow falls not to the ground without our Father.
15:
The historian now draws back the curtain: “the LORD had told Samuel in his ear a day before Saul came.” The Hebrew idiom fascinates every witness. Ellicott translates literally — the Lord “had uncovered the ear of Samuel” — and explains the image as “taken from the action of pushing aside the head-dress, in order the more conveniently to whisper some words to the ear”; he counts the phrase among “the few more direct intimations in the sacred records of one of the ways in which the Spirit of God communicated Divine thoughts to the human spirit”: the Eternal Spirit whispering to a man. Gill hears the same intimacy — God telling Samuel “in a free, familiar, friendly way, as a secret” — and Wesley suggests the whisper came “perhaps by a still small voice.” Clarke, characteristically honest about the limits of exegesis, will only say: “how this communication was made, we cannot tell.”

Matthew Henry spreads the idiom into a little theology of revelation. God spoke in Samuel’s ear “in token of friendship and familiarity, for he revealeth his secret to his servants the prophets” (Amos 3:7) — and there is a lovely reciprocity in it, for Samuel had rehearsed the people’s words “in the ears of the LORD” (1 Sam. 8:21), and now the Lord answers in kind. Henry guesses the whisper came while Samuel was praying in secret for direction, and he allegorises the uncovered ear without apology: we have naturally a covering on our ears, so that we perceive not what God says (Job 33:14); when God will manifest himself to a soul he uncovers the ear, takes the veil from off the heart (2 Cor. 3:16). Revelation, in this figure, is less God raising his voice than God removing our deafness.

The timing carries its own comfort. God told Samuel the day before, Henry notes, “that it might not be a surprise to him,” and perhaps it was in expectation of the morrow’s guest that Samuel appointed the sacrifice and the feast — imploring God’s blessing on the great transaction while keeping the particular occasion locked in his own breast. Keil and Delitzsch observe that this parenthesis (vv.15–17) is inserted precisely so that the reader shall not mistake the street meeting for luck. The witnesses draw the pastoral conclusion together: the God who governs kingdoms is on whispering terms with his servants. Samuel goes to sleep knowing what to-morrow holds because God treats his prophets not as functionaries but as friends — and the man who daily speaks in God’s ear, they imply, is the man most likely to find God speaking in his.
16:
The word whispered to Samuel is precise: to-morrow, about this time, a man from Benjamin; “thou shalt anoint him to be captain over my people Israel, that he may save my people out of the hand of the Philistines: for I have looked upon my people, because their cry is come unto me.” Clarke fastens on the title: Saul is to be anointed not king but nagid — “captain of the Lord’s host” — and he reminds us that in the ancient world no king was esteemed who was not an able warrior, quoting the verse of Homer that Alexander prized: a king of kings must be “both a good king and a strong spearman.” The office, as God defines it, is military and ministerial at once: a deliverer set over the Lord’s own people, not a proprietor of them.

The naming of the Philistines raises a difficulty the grammatical-historical witnesses take seriously: had not Samuel’s victory at Mizpeh already subdued them (1 Sam. 7:13)? Ellicott answers that the earlier statement describes the beginning of a decline, not an annihilation: the Philistine power was broken, “but by no means destroyed,” their raids harassed Israel through Saul’s reign and into David’s, and this pressing need for a younger, more warlike leader than the old prophet-judge was the immediate spur of the people’s prayer for a king. Wesley explains why God names the Philistines rather than the Ammonites then mustering: Ammon was “a land-flood, soon up, and soon down again,” but the Philistines were Israel’s constant and nearest enemies, the ones they most dreaded — and from these Saul did in measure save them, “and would have saved them much more, if his and the people’s sins had not hindered.”

The tenderness of the oracle moves Matthew Henry most. Though the demand for a king was a provocation, God speaks here as if wooing: “even in wrath he remembers mercy.” He calls Israel again and again my people — “though a peevish and provoking people, yet mine still”; he sends them a captain to save them, which was more than many of them aimed at in desiring a king; and he does it “with a gracious respect to them and to their cry: I have looked upon my people, and their cry has come unto me” — gratifying them, says Henry, “as the tender mother humours the froward child, lest it should break its heart.” He passes on Bishop Patrick’s fine observation: though God would not hear their cry against the oppression of the kings they insisted on (1 Sam. 8:18), he was so gracious as to make those very kings instruments of deliverance from their neighbours — more than they had reason to expect. Gill closes the circle of providence: the man himself will arrive with no design of his own, expecting at most news of his father’s asses, “merely directed by the providence of God.” The verse thus holds together what Israel’s request had torn apart: a king granted in displeasure, yet given in love.
17:
The moment the tall stranger comes into view, the inner voice speaks again: “Behold the man whom I spake to thee of! this same shall reign over my people.” Ellicott reconstructs the psychology of the instant with care. This verse follows straight upon v.14 — the intervening verses being parenthetical — and as Samuel, on his way to the height, is met by the young giant, the seer silently asks his Master, “Lord, is this then he of whom Thou whisperest me yesterday?” The words “Behold the man” are “the silent answer of God to the silent prayer of His old servant.” Gill supplies the natural side of the same moment: Samuel could not but notice Saul for his height, and the suspicion that this was the man would rise of itself, since this was the very hour God had fixed; yet God leaves nothing to inference, and speaks “that he might be assured it was he, and be left at no uncertainty about it.” Clarke steps back in wonder at the whole exchange: “what an intimate communion must Samuel have held with his God! A constant familiarity seems to have existed between them.”

The verb of the oracle detains the exegetes, and rewards them. What the Authorised Version renders “shall reign” is, Keil and Delitzsch note, the Hebrew atsar, to coerce, to hold in with authority; Ellicott translates “shall control, or shall restrain,” and hears in the word a forecast of the character of Saul’s government: “it was a word which — looking on to Saul’s future reign — represented it as a stern, severe rule.” Matthew Henry converts the philology into political doctrine with a phrase that has outlived him: this is the man that shall restrain my people, “for magistrates are heirs of restraint.” Rule, as heaven describes it here, is not indulgence of a nation’s wishes but the curbing of its disorders; Israel asked for a figurehead of grandeur, and God, even in granting the request, defines the office as discipline.

The witnesses linger, finally, on the phrase “over my people.” Twice in two verses God has claimed the nation as his own, and the claim frames the grant: Saul receives a stewardship, not a possession. Samuel, watching the man approach who is to supersede him, hears the Lord describe Israel as still my people — governed yesterday by a judge, to-morrow by a king, but belonging in either polity to God alone. That, the commentators suggest, is why the old prophet can greet his replacement without a shadow of grievance: the kingdom changing hands beneath him has never for a moment left the hands that hold both king and seer. The scene closes with the two men face to face in the gate, one knowing all, the other about to learn it.
18:
The irony of the meeting is exquisite, and Matthew Henry makes the most of it: Saul draws near to Samuel and asks him, “Tell me, I pray thee, where the seer’s house is” — not knowing that he is talking to the seer. See, says Henry, “how mean a figure Samuel made, though so great a man”: he took no state, had no attendants, no ensigns of honour carried before him, no distinguishing habit even on a festival day, but appeared so much a common person that Saul, though told he should meet him, addressed him “as if he looked more like a porter than a prophet.” Thus, Henry concludes, “is great worth oftentimes hidden under a very despicable appearance.” The judge of Israel walks his own streets indistinguishable from his townsmen — a fact the witnesses set beside the towering, kingly figure of Saul with evident relish: the man who looks like a king is nothing yet; the man who looks like nobody carries the word of God.

Henry adds a more inward stroke: “Samuel knew him before he knew Samuel,” and makes it a figure of grace — “though all that are called to the kingdom of glory are brought to know God, yet first they were known of him” (Gal. 4:9). Saul comes asking directions of a stranger who has known his name, his errand, and his destiny since yesterday. Every seeker, the Puritan implies, re-enacts the scene: we inquire after God with the awkwardness of men who would not recognise him in the road, while he has long since prepared the feast and reserved the portion.

The critics attend to a smaller matter: where exactly did this happen? The Hebrew says Saul drew near “in the gate”; the Septuagint reads “in the midst of the city.” Ellicott thinks the Greek may preserve the original, a scribe having easily written “gate” for “city.” Keil and Delitzsch defend the received text against Thenius’s proposal to emend v.14 to match: entering a town is done precisely through its gate, so the two statements agree as they stand — the phrase “in the gate” simply defines more exactly the general expression “they were entering the city.” Gill harmonises in his own fashion: the encounter fell either at the door of Samuel’s house or within the city gate as the seer passed out toward the high place. The dispute is minute, but its very minuteness is instructive: the witnesses cared enough about this narrative to argue over a doorway, because in this doorway the era of the judges and the era of the kings stood, for one moment, face to face — the unknown king asking his way of the unrecognised prophet.
19:
“I am the seer.” Gill is careful to clear Samuel of vanity: the words were spoken “not as boasting of his character and office, or in the pride and vanity of his mind, but merely for information sake” — the stranger had asked for the seer’s house, and the plainest answer was the man himself. What follows the disclosure is a cascade of honours, and Keil and Delitzsch parse them exactly. “Go up before me unto the high place”: letting a person walk in front, they note, “was a sign of great esteem”; and the grammar shifts tellingly from singular to plural — Samuel bids Saul alone precede him, but says “ye shall eat with me to day,” expressly sweeping the servant into the invitation as well. Ellicott traces the same courtesies: the singular “go thou up,” a distinguished honour “from one of Samuel’s rank to a young unknown wayfarer”; the plural welcome to the banquet; then the singular again for the promise, “I will tell thee all that is in thine heart.”

That last phrase divides the interpreters gently. It might seem to mean “all that troubles thee” — but Keil and Delitzsch reject this, since Samuel relieved Saul’s one anxiety, the asses, immediately and unasked; the phrase means rather “the thoughts of thy heart generally,” and the disclosure is designed “to prove to him that he was a prophet.” Ellicott reads the promise more romantically: on the morrow the seer will show this young Benjamite his own “secret thoughts and aspirations,” the stirrings Saul himself has hardly owned. Either way the effect is the same — Saul is to discover that he stands before a man to whom his inside is visible; and, as Clarke observes with his usual economy, “this declaration would prepare Saul for the communications afterwards made”: a man convinced that the prophet reads hearts will believe the prophet when he speaks of crowns.

Ellicott supplies the interpretive key to Samuel’s whole procedure here. The old seer’s words were “vague and indefinite, certainly” — a dinner invitation, a promise of disclosures, a hint of destiny — but they were deliberately so: they were “intended to draw him away from thinking about the asses and the little matters which hitherto had filled his life, and to lift him up to higher thoughts and aspirations.” Samuel is conducting an education, hour by hour: first the guest must be detached from his errand, then dignified before witnesses, then, in private and at dawn, told the word of God. The witnesses admire the pedagogy and commend it to every minister: souls called to great things are seldom stormed; they are courted — fed, honoured, and gradually turned from the lost asses of their old preoccupations toward what God has prepared for them, and “to-morrow I will let thee go.”
20:
Samuel’s first gift to Saul is relief: “as for thine asses that were lost three days ago, set not thy mind on them; for they are found.” Gill notes Kimchi’s reckoning of the “three days” and, more importantly, the apologetic force of the sentence: Samuel names the asses, their loss, and its date “before ever he said a word to him about them,” which “must at once convince him that he was a true prophet, and prepare him to give credit to all that he should hereafter say.” Clarke reads it identically — “thus he shows him that he knew what was in his heart” — and Matthew Henry ascends from the sign to its doctrine: if a man of God can answer the question we have not yet asked, “much more doth God himself understand our thoughts afar off.”

Then comes the sentence on which the witnesses genuinely divide: “and on whom is all the desire of Israel? Is it not on thee, and on all thy father’s house?” The majority take “the desire of Israel” as the thing Israel is desiring — the king. Wesley paraphrases: who is he that shall be that which all Israel desire to have, namely, a king? — adding, from the second clause, that the honour is designed “for thee, and, after thy death, for thy family or posterity, if by thy sin thou dost not cut off the entail” — a condition that hangs over the house of Saul like a drawn sword. Henry expands in the same sense: is it not a king they are set upon, “and there is never a man in Israel that will suit them as thou wilt”; and Clarke reports that Saul himself so understood it, as implying his election. But Ellicott, following the Vulgate’s optima quaeque Israel and Luther’s “the best in Israel,” argues the words do not signify what Israel desires but “all that it possesses of what is precious or worth desiring” — everything desirable in Israel is henceforth thine and thy father’s house’s. On his reading Samuel is not naming the crown but opening a vista.

The two readings converge in their purpose. Whether the seer said “Israel’s longing has settled on thee” or “Israel’s treasure shall be thine,” the sentence was, as Ellicott puts it, an “obscure dark word” designed to amaze — and it did: the young man, “full of wonderment and awe,” will answer in the next verse out of his tribe’s smallness. The witnesses note too what Samuel is doing pastorally: first he empties Saul’s hands of his little care — set not thy mind on them — and only then loads them with an immense one. It is, they suggest, the order of all divine calling: God quiets our anxieties about the lost asses of our life precisely so that we may hear, undistracted, the far heavier thing he has to say to us; and it does not appear, Henry adds, that the country had any eye on Saul for the government — the desire of Israel was resting on a man Israel had never heard of.
21:
Saul’s answer is the best sentence he ever speaks: am not I a Benjamite, of the smallest of the tribes of Israel, and my family the least of all the families of the tribe of Benjamin? “wherefore then speakest thou so to me?” The witnesses first verify the arithmetic of his humility. Wesley recalls that Benjamin had been cut down to six hundred men in the war that followed the crime of Gibeah (Judges 20:46–48), “which blow they never recovered,” so that the tribe “was scarce reckoned as an entire tribe, but only as a remnant of a tribe,” half-merged into Judah. Clarke agrees that Benjamin “had not yet recovered its strength after the ruinous war,” and Matthew Henry adds the family register: Benjamin was the youngest of Jacob’s sons, “a little one” (Gen. 44:20), and Saul’s own house, for all Kish’s substance, held no place of honour or trust even within its diminished tribe. The modesty, in other words, is not a pose; it is a sober description of his standing — though Henry slyly notes the tension with v.1, where the historian calls his father a mighty man of power: Saul speaks more diminishingly of his family than the facts require, as humble men do.

The witnesses then set the answer in its noble lineage. Henry points to Gideon, who protested in nearly the same words — my clan is the poorest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father’s house (Judges 6:15) — and draws the maxim: “a humble disposition is a good presage of preferment.” God’s chosen instruments habitually enter their calling protesting their smallness; it is the self-nominated who should be feared. Clarke pronounces the speech “exceedingly modest,” Saul “becomingly humble” — and then appends the sentence that turns the verse tragic in retrospect: “but who can bear elevation and prosperity?” The reader who knows the end of the story hears both notes at once: the genuine lowliness of the young farmer at the feast, and the terrible ease with which lowliness evaporates when the head that owned it wears a crown.

There is, the witnesses agree, a theology in the choice itself. That the first king should come from the smallest tribe — a tribe once nearly erased for its sin, surviving as a remnant of six hundred — is of a piece with the God who raises the poor out of the dust (1 Sam. 2:8) and gives more abundant honour to the part which lacked (1 Cor. 12:24). Israel asked for a king like the nations, and God, even in granting them their carnal wish, kept his own signature on the grant: he took their portly, admirable king from the humblest possible address, so that no flesh — not even the flesh they idolised — should glory in his presence.
22:
Samuel makes no reply to Saul’s astonished question — and Ellicott reads the silence as strategy: the seer “merely wished, in the first instance, to awaken new and grander thoughts and aspirations in this young heart,” and so, without a word of explanation, he conducts his guests up to the feast. The answer to “wherefore speakest thou so to me?” is not an argument but a seat. The witnesses then correct the furniture of the Authorised Version: the “parlour,” Ellicott notes, is an unfortunate rendering of the Hebrew lishkah, the cell or chamber attached to the high place for the sacrificial meals; Keil and Delitzsch describe it as the apartment prepared for the feast, where some thirty persons — “no doubt the most distinguished men of the city,” the rest of the people probably encamping without — sat down together, and Saul and his servant were placed “at the upper end among those who had been invited.”

The detail that the servant shared the honour strikes several witnesses. Keil and Delitzsch credit “the simple customs of antiquity,” under which the attendant of a guest was a guest; Ellicott adds the old tradition that this servant was Doeg the Edomite, who thus “had a foretaste of his future position” among Saul’s great officers of state (1 Sam. 22:18) — a grim garnish to the feast, if the tradition holds. Wesley explains the seating itself as public pedagogy: Samuel set them in the chief place “thereby to raise their expectation, and to prepare them for giving that honour to Saul, which his approaching dignity required.” The thirty notables of Ramah are being taught, before they know it, to defer to their king.

Matthew Henry finds the glory of the verse in the host. The advancement of Saul would be the deposing of Samuel; yet the good prophet, “so far from envying him, or bearing him any ill-will for it,” was “the first and forwardest man to do him honour, in compliance with the will of God.” If this be the man whom God has chosen, then — though he is none of Samuel’s friends or confidants, though his rise is Samuel’s eclipse — he is heartily welcome to his table, nay, to his bosom; Henry hears in the scene an anticipation of “Give this man place” (Luke 14:9), with the incumbent himself doing the giving. The witnesses commend the picture to every servant of God who must watch his own succession arrive: Samuel seats his replacement above him at his own feast, and in doing so shows that a man who holds his office from God can lay it down with both hands. Meanwhile the guest of honour, who came up the hill looking for livestock, sits bewildered at the head of a table he did not know was set for him.
23:
Samuel now speaks to the cook: “Bring the portion which I gave thee, of which I said unto thee, Set it by thee.” The witnesses find the verse small and its implications large. Ellicott states the larger meaning plainly: everything in this meeting — the feast, the seating, the dish — “was arranged for beforehand; every event was foreseen and provided for, even the trivial details; all was symbolical in this preparation for the great change in the constitution of Israel.” The piece of meat set before Saul “was no chance piece”: when the revelation came the day before, Samuel appointed the sacrifice, bade thirty of the chief citizens, and instructed the cook to reserve the choicest portion for a guest whose face he had never seen. Providence, having whispered in the prophet’s ear, now works through a kitchen.

The philologists attend to their trades. Clarke examines the word rendered “cook”: the Hebrew tabbach comes from a root meaning to slay or butcher, so that “probably the butcher is here meant” — the man who had slaughtered the sacrifice kept charge of its portions; he notes too the feminine form of the same word for the “cooks” whom the coming king would conscript, as Samuel had warned in 1 Samuel 8:13. Keil and Delitzsch, looking ahead to the dish itself, sift the proposals for “that which was upon it”: not the broth poured over it, as Dathe and Maurer imagined, nor the kidney, as Thenius supposed — for the kidneys of every slain offering were burned upon the altar (Lev. 3:4) — but, with Luther, what was attached to the leg, the fat left upon the flesh that was not destined for the altar. Gill is content to identify the addressee: the man “that dressed and prepared the food for the entertainment of the guests.”

Out of these particulars the witnesses draw a single, satisfying lesson: the God of Israel prepares in advance, and in detail. A day before Saul knew Samuel existed as anything but a name, his place at table was assigned and his dinner was under orders. Ellicott’s word “symbolical” governs the whole: the reserved portion is the visible pledge that Saul’s destiny has been reserved — settled, set by, waiting only for the appointed hour and the appointed guest. The commentators invite the reader to apply the figure widely: what God intends for a man is kept for him with the same quiet exactness, through agents as unlikely as a butcher at a country high place, so that when he finally sits down, bewildered, at providence’s table, he finds that he was expected all along.
24:
The dish is set before Saul — the shoulder, “and that which was upon it” — and the witnesses fall into two instructive debates. The first concerns who speaks the words that follow. The Authorised Version, with the Septuagint and Vulgate, gives them to Samuel; but Ellicott, following Dean Payne Smith, insists “Samuel’s name is not given in the Hebrew,” that the Syriac and Chaldee make the cook the speaker, and that the verb is a participle, not an imperative: the cook lifts the shoulder, sets it before Saul, and chats — “Behold that which hath been reserved is set before thee; eat: for it hath been kept for thee unto the appointed time.” The ellipsis then resolves itself: it was Samuel who, when telling the cook of his invitation, said, “I have invited the people,” and ordered the portion kept. “The chat of the cook,” Payne Smith observes, “is entirely after the manner of ancient times, and would show Saul how completely his coming had been foreseen and provided for.” Wesley represents the traditional reading: Samuel himself says, in effect, when I first invited the people, “I then bade the cook reserve this part for thy use.”

The second debate concerns the shoulder itself. Clarke will not allow that it was merely the best cut: it was chosen “because it was an emblem of the government to which he was now called,” and he quotes Isaiah 9:6 — “the government shall be upon his shoulder.” Matthew Henry works the symbolism doubly: the right shoulder of the peace-offerings belonged to the priests (Lev. 7:32); the left, he supposes, was the portion of the presiding guest — Samuel’s own accustomed mess — “so that his giving it to Saul now was an implicit resignation of his place to him”; and the shoulder denotes strength as the breast denotes affection: the king must bear the government on his shoulder and carry the people in his bosom. Gill canvasses the alternatives — sauce or garnish, the thigh of the Targum, or the breast — and suggests that in those unsettled times Samuel, though a Levite, may have officiated as priest, so that the right shoulder was his to bestow: Saul, fed from the priest’s portion, is taught that he must “live in friendship with the priests of the Lord.” Keil and Delitzsch, more cautious, note only that the earlier commentators chose the left because the right fell to the priests, and identify what lay upon it as the sacrificial fat.

Beneath both disputes lies an agreement the witnesses all voice: “so Saul did eat with Samuel that day,” and every mouthful was a sermon. Whether by prophet’s lips or cook’s chatter, Saul learned that he had been expected — invited before he set out, provided for before he was found. The reserved shoulder, kept “unto this time,” is the whole chapter in a single dish: a destiny appointed, guarded, and served at the hour God had said.
25:
The feast over, prophet and farmer come down from the high place, and Samuel “communed with Saul upon the top of the house.” Clarke furnishes the setting: the flat roofs of the East were the family’s evening room, where people “walked, talked, and frequently slept, for the sake of fresh and cooling air”; Gill traces the descent from the height and the climb back to the hill-built city. But what was said on that rooftop divides the witnesses into two camps. Wesley answers without hesitation: they communed “concerning the kingdom designed for him by God”; and Matthew Henry fills in the imagined conversation — Samuel told him the whole story of the people’s desire of a king, satisfied him that he was the man, and met Saul’s scruple about supplanting the prophet with every assurance of his willingness to resign. Against this, Keil and Delitzsch insist the conversation “did not refer of course to the call of Samuel to the royal dignity, for that was not made known to him as a word of Jehovah till the following day” (v.27); the evening talk was preparation only. Ellicott agrees, adopting von Gerlach’s reconstruction: the old man spoke of “the sad religious and political decline of the people of God,” the opposition of the heathen nations, the causes of Israel’s impotence, “the necessity of a religious change in the people, and of a leader thoroughly obedient to the Lord” — the rooftop discourse forming the bridge between the dark hints at the feast and the anointing at dawn.

Ellicott also reports a textual curiosity that bears on the question. The Septuagint replaces “communed with Saul upon the top of the house” with “they strewed a couch for Saul on the top of the house, and he lay down” — no conversation at all. The Chaldee and Syriac stand with the Hebrew, and Ellicott judges the Greek “apparently a correction” by translators who could not understand an evening of communing when the formal announcement waited for morning: their difficulty is the very question — why did Samuel not tell Saul of God’s intention that night? — which the von Gerlach reading answers: because the word of God deserved its own hour, apart, in the morning, with no feast behind it and no roof of a house but the open sky.

All the witnesses, whichever side they take, are drawn by the picture itself: the last judge of Israel and its first king, sitting into the night on a flat roof in Ramah, an old man pouring into a young one everything the coming reign would need. Henry’s admiration serves for the whole cloud: Samuel, whom this guest would displace, was “so far from envying him” that he gave him his table, his roof, his counsel and his blessing — the outgoing servant of God equipping the incoming with a generosity that has no rival in the politics of the nations.
26:
“And they arose early: and it came to pass about the spring of the day…” The witnesses first settle what happened, then disagree — charmingly — about how anyone slept. Ellicott corrects the English rendering, which seems to make two events of rising early and the call at dawn: in Hebrew narration “the second clause simply relates the same event as the first, only with greater detail,” so the sense is, “they arose early, namely, when the morning dawned; Samuel called to Saul upon the roof, Get up, that I may send thee away.” Keil and Delitzsch supply the domestic geography: Samuel called from below within the house up to the roof, “where Saul was probably sleeping upon the balcony” — the guest-chamber of the East, as in the prophet’s room of 2 Kings 4:10. Clarke reads it the same way: Saul had no doubt slept there all night, and at break of day Samuel called him up; there was no summoning him to the house-top a second time.

Whether he slept is another matter. Ellicott imagines Saul, “evidently weary after the exciting scene and revelations of the day before,” sleeping soundly, probably heavily, on his couch. Gill transmits the opposite conjecture from Abarbinel: they rose early because neither of them had been able to sleep at all — “not Samuel, for thinking what he was to do the next morning, anoint Saul king over Israel; nor Saul, for what Samuel had hinted to him about the desire of all Israel being upon him, and for the honour done him at the feast, and because of the conversation they had together afterwards.” The disagreement cannot be settled and does not need to be; between them the two guesses map the night perfectly — the body’s exhaustion and the mind’s tumult — and either way the dawn finds both men ready.

The witnesses dwell on the urgency of the hour. It is Samuel who ends the night: “Up, that I may send thee away.” The old prophet, who has carried the secret for two days, will not let the sun find it untold; the word of God is burning toward its utterance, and the anointing must come before breakfast, before the town wakes, before anything can intrude between the message and the man. There is a pastoral picture here the commentators quietly admire: the seer as God’s alarm-clock, rousing a sleeping man to a destiny he could not have dreamed toward. Saul rises from a borrowed bed on a stranger’s roof for the last morning of his private life; by the time the dew dries he will be the anointed of the Lord, and the two of them go out together, he and Samuel, abroad into the breaking day.
27:
At the edge of the town Samuel stops the little procession: “Bid the servant pass on before us … but stand thou still a while, that I may shew thee the word of God.” The witnesses agree on the reason for the dismissal and differ only in emphasis. Wesley gives the fullest politics of the privacy: Samuel had hitherto concealed the matter “lest he should be thought now to impose a king upon them, as before he denied one to them”; and the secrecy served a deeper design — that when the public lot fell on Saul in the next chapter, “it might appear that the kingdom was given to Saul by God's destination, and not by Samuel's contrivance.” The prophet protects both the king’s legitimacy and his own integrity by making sure no human witness can say the choice was arranged. Matthew Henry adds the sacredness of the act itself: Samuel sent the servant ahead “that they might be private,” and there anointed him, “for he would not jest with that sacred rite” — and Henry cannot leave the anointing without its gospel echo: it is by the unction of the Holy One that believers know all things (1 John 2:27).

Ellicott gathers up the whole two days into this moment: the honours at the feast, the hospitality, the long rooftop instruction — “the meaning of all that had happened to him on the day before was now to be revealed.” The “word of God,” as Wesley defines it, is “a message delivered to me from God, which now I shall impart to thee”; Clarke calls it “the word, the counsel or design, of the Lord,” to be unfolded in the anointing of 1 Samuel 10:1. And Ellicott, noting that the historian tells us nothing of Saul’s state of mind under this avalanche, points forward to the promise of the Spirit in the next chapter and coins the chapter’s finest aphorism: “when new duties are imposed by God, He never forgets to bestow the gift of new powers.”

Henry closes the chapter for the whole cloud of witnesses with his unforgettable application. Saul was perhaps the only man who ever went out to seek asses and literally found a kingdom; but many have set out to seek riches or pleasures “and have been guided to places where they found salvation for their souls,” met by those who addressed them as if aware of the secrets of their lives, and led seriously to regard the word of the Lord. If that has been our case, says Henry, though our worldly plans have not prospered, let us not care: the Lord has given us, or prepared us for, what is far better. The chapter that began with lost livestock ends with a man standing still at daybreak to receive the word of God — the posture, the witnesses imply, in which every calling is finally conferred.
